questions.question: What is the dominant access pattern of a URL shortener, and what does it imply?
options: Very read-heavy (~100:1); the design centers on caching and read replicas
Balanced reads and writes; no caching needed
Mostly range queries; needs range partitioning
questions.explanation: Links are created once and followed many times, so reads dominate writes ~100:1. That single fact makes caching (and read replicas) the heart of the design — the redirect read path is where the system lives or dies.

questions.question: Why is base62 (counter-based) key generation collision-free without any collision check?
options: Each code is the base62 encoding of a unique integer ID, so distinct IDs always produce distinct codes
questions.explanation: Encoding a guaranteed-unique counter/ID in base62 yields a guaranteed-unique code — there's a one-to-one mapping. No collision check or retry is needed, unlike random/hash-based codes which can collide and must be checked.

questions.question: How many unique codes does a 7-character base62 scheme provide?
questions.explanation: 62^7 ≈ 3.5 trillion combinations using [0-9a-zA-Z]. That's far more than enough for billions of links, which is why 6-7 characters is the typical short-code length.

questions.question: What is the real bottleneck of a URL shortener, and the deep-dive that addresses it?
options: Key generation; solved by a faster CPU
The read (redirect) path; solved with caching and read replicas
Writes; solved by removing the database
questions.explanation: With reads outnumbering writes ~100:1, the redirect read path is the bottleneck. The deep dive is caching: a Redis cache of short→long serves ~99% of redirects in ~1ms, and read replicas (plus CDN-cached 301s) handle the rest, shielding the database.

questions.question: What is the tradeoff between returning a 301 vs a 302 redirect?
options: 301 is cacheable by browsers/CDNs (offloads you but you can't easily count clicks); 302 routes every click through you (enables analytics) at higher load
questions.explanation: A 301 (permanent) can be cached downstream, so many clicks never reach you — great for offload, but you lose per-click counting and easy retargeting. A 302 (temporary) sends every click through your service, enabling analytics at the cost of more load. Choose by whether you need analytics or raw offload.
